content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 26, 2000


Patricia Williams
Member, State of California
Employment Training Panel
6605 Beford Avenue
Los Angeles, California 90056

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-00-072

Dear Ms. Williams:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

How would the Act's post-employment restrictions affect you if you left the State of California Employment Training Panel ("ETP")?

CONCLUSION

For one year after you leave your position as a member of the ETP, you will be prohibited from making any formal or informal appearances before ETP or its departments to influence any administrative or legislative action of the agency, or any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a contract. You will also be permanently barred from "switching sides" in a proceeding — under the Act's permanent ban, you cannot work on any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding, that you participated in while you were a board member at ETP.

_______________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18996, of the California Code of Regulations. Because this letter provides general guidance about the Act's post-employment restrictions, it is indicated by the file number prefix "I." Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulations 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-00-072
Page 2

FACTS

You are currently a member of the State of California Employment Training Panel
("ETP"). You have served on the ETP for 17 years, first appointed by Willie Brown and later by
Antonio Villaraigosa.

The Employment Training Panel reimburses California companies for training programs.
Not all employers are eligible for funding from ETP, only those who pay into the State's
Employment Training Fund and are hiring or training unemployed workers or meet certain other
eligibility requirements. An employer who is seeking funding must submit a request for
eligibility determination. A final agreement between ETP and an employer to fund training
programs has a statutory duration of two years.

Until recently, you were employed by the UAW Labor Employment Training Corp.
("UAW-LETC"), a nonprofit organization. You served as president and chief executive officer
of the UAW-LETC. A new president and CEO of UAW-LETC was brought in and you then
served as senior vice president and director of marketing until your employment with UAW-
LETC ended in early April.

You called to inform us that your employment with UAW-LETC ended in early April.²
However, this letter provides general guidance as to how the Act's post-employment restrictions
will affect you in the future, if you decide to leave the state ETP.

ANALYSIS

Officials who have left state service are subject to two types of restrictions under the Act.
The first is a one-year prohibition on making any appearance before your former agency for
compensation for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or any action
involving contracts. The second is a permanent prohibition on advising or representing any
person for compensation in any judicial or other proceeding (including a contract) in which you
participated while in state service.

1. One-Year Ban

This post-employment restriction was added to the Act to restrict individuals who have
left state service from capitalizing on the expertise and relationships they developed at their
former agency for a period of one year. Section 87406(d)(1) of the Act provides that:

___________________________

² The facts include those set forth in your request for advice dated March 13, 2000, and those obtained
from a telephone conversation with you on April 7, 2000.

File No. I-00-072
Page 3

"No designated employee of a state administrative agency, any
officer, employee, or consultant of a state administrative agency
who holds a position which entails the making, or participation in
the making, of decisions which may foreseeably have a material
effect on any financial interest, and no member of a state
administrative agency, for a period of one year after leaving office
or employment, shall, for compensation, act as agent or attorney
for, or otherwise represent, any other person, by making any
formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral or written
communication, before any state administrative agency, or officer
or employee thereof, for which he or she worked or represented
during the 12 months before leaving office or employment, if the
appearance or communication is made for the purpose of
influencing administrative or legislative action, or influencing any
action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment,
awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or
the sale or purchase of goods or property."

As a member of the Employment Training Panel, you are covered by the Act's one-year
ban. Pursuant to section 87406, for one year after the date you leave ETP, you may not, for
compensation, act as representative or agent for any person before the ETP for the purpose of
influencing³ administrative⁴ or legislative⁵ action or any action or proceeding involving the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale
or purchase of goods or property. (Section 87406(d)(1).) The prohibition of Section 87406 on
making an appearance before ETP extends to the various units and regional offices of the agency
(regulation 18746.1(b)(6)(B)), and the one-year ban begins when an employee has permanently
left state service (regulation 18746.1(b)(1)).

³ "Influencing legislative or administrative action" includes influencing by any means, including but not
limited to the provision or use of information, statistics, studies or analyses. (Section 82032.)

⁴ "Administrative action" is defined in Section 82002 as the proposal, drafting, development or
consideration, amendment, enactment or defeat by any state agency of any rule, regulation or other action in any
rate-making proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding, including any proceeding regarding adoption of
regulations governed by Government Code 11340 et seq.

⁵ Section 82037 defines "legislative action" as the drafting, introduction, consideration, modification,
enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the Legislature or by
either house or any committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, or by a member or employee of the
Legislature acting in his or her official capacity. "Legislative action" also means the action of the Governor in
approving or vetoing any bill.

File No. I-00-072
Page 4

The one year ban regulates specific conduct—appearing before or communicating with
one's former agency for the purpose of influencing certain proceedings. If you leave your
position at the ETP, for one year after your departure you are prohibited from making any formal
or informal appearances before the agency or its departments to influence ETP action involving
the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a contract. An appearance or
communication includes, but is not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person,
corresponding with in writing or by electronic transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering
or sending any communication. (Regulation 18746.2(a).)

However, communications to administrative agencies which are not for the purpose of
influencing administrative or legislative action or action involving contracts are not restricted by
the one-year ban. For example, if you resigned from the ETP, you could attend informational
meetings at ETP offices, or request information from the agency concerning existing laws,
regulations, or policies, so long as you did not attempt to influence administrative or legislative
action or action involving contracts. (Regulation 18746.2(b).)

In addition, if you leave ETP and work for a private employer, for one year you could not
be identified as the sender of communications to ETP regarding action on a funding contract
between ETP and an employer. In addition, you could not personally contact or meet with any
official or employee of ETP concerning a contract. (Marovich Advice Letter, No. A-99-131; and
Bohart Advice Letter, No. I-99-231.) On the other hand, the one-year prohibition would not
prohibit you from drafting documents on behalf of an employer to be submitted to ETP, as long
as you are not identified in connection with the communication. (Marovich Advice Letter,
supra.) Moreover, you could use your expertise to advise a private employer or client about ETP
procedures, contract approval process, etc., as long as you are not identified with the employer or
client's efforts to influence ETP action on a contract. (Pratt Advice Letter, No. A-95-386; and
Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.)

2. Permanent Prohibition

The Act contains a permanent prohibition on former state officials' involvement in certain
proceedings in which they participated while working for the state. Sections 87401 and 87402
provide:

"No former state administrative official, after the termination of
his or her employment or term of office, shall for compensation act
as agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person
(other than the State of California) before any court or state
administrative agency or any officer or employee thereof by
making any formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral
or written communication with the intent to influence, in

File No. I-00-072
Page 5

connection with any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding if
both of the following apply:
(a) The State of California is a party or has a direct and
substantial interest.
(b) The proceeding is one in which the former state
administrative official participated." (Section 87401.)

"No former state administrative official, after the termination of
his or her employment or term of office shall for compensation aid,
advise, counsel, consult or assist in representing any other person
(except the State of California) in any proceeding in which the
official would be prohibited from appearing under Section 87401."
(Section 87402.)

The ETP is a state administrative agency. (Section 87400(a).) Section
87400(b) defines a "state administrative official" as every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state
administrative agency who as part of his or her official responsibilities engages in any judicial,
quasi-judicial or other proceeding in other than a purely clerical, secretarial or ministerial
capacity. As a member of the ETP, you are considered a state administrative official.

The permanent ban is limited to judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceedings in which you
participated. Section 87400 provides:

"(c) 'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any
proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other determination,
contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation,
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties
in any court or state administrative agency, including but not
limited to any proceeding governed by Chapter 5 (commencing
with Section 11500) of Division 3 of Title 2 of the Government
Code.
(d) 'Participated' means to have taken part personally and
substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal
written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis,
investigation or use of confidential information as an officer or
employee, but excluding approval, disapproval or rendering of
legal advisory opinions to departmental or agency staff which do
not involve a specific party or parties." (Emphasis added.)

Under Section 87400(c) set forth above, a "proceeding" includes any particular matter or
contract involving a specific party or parties in a state administrative agency. ETP enters into
contracts with eligible companies or training agencies for funding job training. A request for

File No. I-00-072
Page 6

eligibility determination is the first step in the Employment Training Panel contracting process. The request for eligibility determination is the initial form the company or training agency seeking ETP funding fills out and sends to ETP's office in Sacramento for review. If the company or training agency is found eligible, it has one year to turn in an application for funding. ETP field office staff conduct a site visit and help the company develop its application for funding. Following negotiation between ETP field office staff and the company, a final agreement is submitted to the Panel for approval at one of its monthly meetings. The request for eligibility determination and the resulting agreement between ETP and a company is considered a proceeding covered by the permanent ban because it involves a state agency and a specific party. (Negrete Advice Letter, No. A-99-177; Pratt Advice Letter, No. A-95-386; Hargrove Advice Letter, No. A-93-201.)

If you resign from the ETP, and work to obtain ETP contracts for companies at a private employer, sections 87401 and 87402 would prohibit you from working on the same request for eligibility determination or other contract or proceeding that you worked on or approved while you were a member of the ETP. Thus, if you leave the ETP, you will be permanently prohibited from performing work for a private employer on a contract that you worked on or approved as a member of the ETP.6

However, sections 87401 and 87402 would not restrict your ability to participate in new proceedings. To the extent that your marketing work with a private employer involved contracts with the ETP which you did not work on or approve as a member of the ETP,7 or involved new contracts with the ETP, the permanent ban would not prohibit you from working on those. For example, after the expiration of the one-year ban discussed above, you could work for a private employer on negotiating a company's new request for eligibility determination with ETP.

3. Prospective Employment

The Act also prohibits a state official from making, participating in making, or using his or her official position to influence any governmental decision that directly relates to an entity with whom the official is negotiating, or has any arrangement concerning, prospective employment. (Section 87407; Regulation 18747.) A public official is "negotiating" employment when he or she interviews or discusses an offer of employment with an employer. (Regulation 18747(c)(1).) While you are serving on the ETP, you should be careful not to make, participate in making, or influence governmental decisions directly relating to a company or nonprofit

6 The prohibition of Section 87401 applies to compensation for representation of any person other than the state of California.

7 A supervisor is deemed to have participated in any proceeding which was pending before the official's agency and which was under his or her supervisory authority. (Regulation 18741.1)

File No. I-00-072
Page 7

organization if you are negotiating or have an arrangement concerning prospective employment
with that entity.

I have enclosed a copy of the Commission's fact sheet on post-employment restrictions
for your information. If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: Hyla P. Wagner
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure

,"q.r"rio, with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 831 14; Regulation
I 832e(c)(3).)

Officials who have left state service are subject to two types of restrictions under the Act.
The first is a one-year prohibition on making any appearance before your former agency for
compensation for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or any action
involving contracts. The second is a permanent prohibition on advising or representing any
person for compensation in any judicial or other proceeding (including a contract) in which you
participated while in state service.
1. One-Year Ban
This post-employment restriction was added to the Act to restrict individuals who have
left state service from capitaLizing on the expertise and relationships they developed at their
former agency for a period of one year. Section 87406(d)(1) of the Act provides that:

or employee thereof, for which he or she worked or represented
during the 12 months before leaving office or employment, if the
appearance or communication is made for the purpose of
influencing administrative or legislative action, or influencing any
action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment,
awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grilt, or contract, or
the sale or purchase of goods or property."
As a member of the Employment Training Panel, you are covered by the Act's one-year
ban. Pursuant to section 87406, for one year after the date you.leave ETP, you may not, for
compensation, act as representative or agent for any person before the ETP for the purpose of
influencing3 administrativea or legislatives action or any action or proceeding involving the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grffit, or conhact, or the sale

regulations govemed by Government Code 11340 et seq.
5 Section 82037 defines "legislative action" as the drafting, intoduction, consideration, modification,
enactnent or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the Legislature or by
either house or any committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereo{ or by a member or employee of the
Legislature acting in his or her official capacity. "Legislative action" also means the action of the Governor in
approving or vetoing any bill.
The one year ban regulates specific conduct-appearing before or communicating with
one's former agency for the purpose of influencing certain proceedings. If you leave your
position at the ETP, for one year after your departure you are prohibited from making any formal
or informal appearances before the agency or its departments to influence ETP action involving
the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a contract. An appearance or
communication includes, but is not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person,
corresponding with in writing or by electronic transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering
or sending any communication. (Regulation ft7a6.2(a).)
However, communications to administrative agencies which are not for the purpose of
influencing administrative or legislative action or action involving contracts are not restricted by
the one-year ban. For example, if you resigned from the ETP, you could attend informational
meetings at ETP offices, or request information from the agency conceming existing laws,
regulations, or policies, so long as you did not attempt to influence administrative or legislative
action or action involving contracts. (Regulation 18746.2@).)
In addition, if you leave ETP and work for a private employer, for one year you could not

between ETP and an employer. In addition, you could not personally contact or meet with any

Bohart Advice Letter, No. I-99-231.) On the other hand, the one-year prohibition would not
prohibit you from drafting documents onbehalf of an employer to be submitted to ETP, as long
as you are not identified in connection with the communication. (Marovich Advice Letter,
supra.) Moreover, you could use your expertise to advise a private employer or client about ETP
procedures, contract approval process, etc., as long as you are not identified with the employer or
client's efforts to influence ETP action on a contract. (Pratt Advice Letter, No. A-95-386; and
Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.)
2. Permanent Prohibition
The Act contains a permanent prohibition on former state officials' involvement in certain
proceedings in which they participated while working for the state. Sections 87401 arlrd87402
provide:
"No former state administrative official, after the termination of
his or her employment or term of office, shall for compensation act
as agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person
(other than the State of California) before any court or state
administrative agency or any officer or employee thereof by
making any formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral
or written communication with the intent to influence, in
connection with any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding if
both of the following apply:
(a) The State of California is a party or has a direct and
substantial interest.
(b) The proceeding is one in which the former state
administrative official participated." (Section 8740 1 .)
'T.{o former state administrative official, after the termination of
his or her employment or term of office shall for compensation aid,
advise, counsel, consult or assist in representing any other person
(except the State of Califomia) in any proceeding in which the
official would be prohibited from appearing under Section 87401."
(Section 87402.)
The ETP is a state administrative agency. (Section 87a00(a).) Section 87400(b) defines

administrative agency who as part of his or her official responsibilities engages in any judicial,
quasi-judicial or other proceeding in other than a purely clerical, secretarial or ministerial

The permanent ban is limited to judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceedings in which you
participated. Section 87400 provides:
"(c) 'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any
proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other determination,
contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation,
a:rest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties
in any court or state administrative agency, including but not
limited to any proceeding governed by Chapter 5 (commencing
with Section t 1500) of Division 3 of Title 2 of the Govemment
Code.
(d) 'Participated' means to have taken part personally and
substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal
written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis,
investigation or use of confidential information as an officer or
employee, but excluding approval, disapproval or rendering of
legal advisory opinions to departmental or agency staff which do
not involve a specific party or parties." (Emphasis added.)
Under Section 87a00(c) set forth above, a "proceeding" includes ar.ry particular matter or
contract involving a specific parfy or parties in a state adminishative agency. ETP enters into
contracts with eligible companies or training agencies for funding job training. A request for
eligibility determination is the first step in the Employment Training Panel contracting process.
The request for eligibility determination is the initial form the company or haining agency
seeking ETP funding fills out and sends to ETP's office in Sacramento for review. If the
company or training agency is found eligible, it has one year to tum in an application for
funding. ETP field office staff conduct a site visit and help the company develop its application
for funding. Following negotiation between ETP field o{fice staffand the company, a final
agreement is submitted to the Panel for approval at one of its monthly meetings. The request for
eligibility determination and the resulting agreement between ETP and a company is considered
a proceeding covered by the permanent bar, because it involves a state agency and a specific
party. (Negrete Advice Letter, No. A-99-177; Pratt Advice Letter, No. A-95-3861' Hargrove
Advice Letter, No. A-93-201.)
If you resign from the ETP, and work to obtain ETP contracts for companies at a private
employer, sections 87401 and87402 would prohibit you from working on the same request for
eligibility determination or other contract or proceeding that you worked on or approved while
you were a member of the ETP. Thus, if you leave the ETP, you will be permanently prohibited
from performing work for a private employer on a contract that you worked on or approved as a
member of the ETP.6
However, sections 87401 md87402 would not restrict your ability to participateinnew
proceedings. To the extent that your marketing work with a private employer involved contracts
with the ETP which yol did not workon or approve as a member of the ETP,7 or involved new
contracts with the ETP, the permanent ban would not prohibit you from working on those. For
example, after the expiration of the one-year ban discussed above, you could work for a private
employer on negotiating a company's new request for eligibility determination with ETP.
3. Prospective Employment
The Act also prohibits a state official from making, participating in making, or using his
or her official position to influence any govemmental decision that directly relates to an entity
with whom the official is negotiating, or has any arangement conceming, prospective
employment. (Section 87407; Regulation t8747.) A public official is "negotiating" employment
when he or she interviews or discusses an offer of employment with an employer. (Regulation
187a7@)(l).) While you are serving on the ETP, you should be careful not to make, participate

agency and which was under his or her supervisory authority. (Regulation l874l.l)

Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18996, of the California Code of Regulations. Because this letter provides general guidance about the Act's post-employment restrictions, it is indicated by the file number prefix "I." Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulations 18329(c)(3).) Until recently, you were employed by the UAW Labor Employment Training Corp. ("UAW-LETC"), a nonprofit organization. You served as president and chief executive officer of the UAW-LETC. A new president and CEO of UAW-LETC was brought in and you then served as senior vice president and director of marketing until your employment with UAW- LETC ended in early April.
